summary: Jeff Atwood argues that password security has shown no improvement over 25 years because the problem is fundamentally human, not technological. He cites research from 1979 and 2002 showing that roughly 30% of passwords can be cracked quickly in both eras. Rather than fighting human nature with complex random passwords like 'wX%95qd!', Atwood advocates for passphrases—longer strings of words that are both more secure and easier to remember. He calls on developers to support passwords of at least 128 unicode characters and to actively educate users that 'password' doesn't mean a single word.
key_points: Password security hasn't improved between 1979 and 2005 because it's a people problem, not a technology problem
Humans are inherently bad at generating randomness, making traditional complex passwords both hard to create and hard to remember
Even the worst passphrase ('this is my secret password') is far more secure than an average single-word password
Developers perpetuate the problem by enforcing short password length limits (8-16 characters)
User education must happen at the point of password creation, with examples of passphrases shown directly in the UI
money_quotes: When there's no measurable improvement in password security between 1979 and 2005, clearly we aren't dealing with a technology problem. We're dealing with a people problem.
Passwords are fundamentally broken because they aren't compatible with typical human behavior.
When humans make passwords, (they) are not very good at making up randomness.
The worst imaginable pass phrase (e.g., 'this is my secret password') is many times more secure than an average single word password (e.g., 'god123'). And it's easier to remember.
The greatest long term security threat isn't hackers. It's the perpetuation of the braindead 8-16 character password length limitation, and the idea that passwords are single words.
We have yet to see a company whose employees don't pick bad passwords.
key_insight: Password security fails because we've been fighting human nature instead of working with it—passphrases align security with how people actually think and remember.
